attributes.stem:
An 81-year-old man presents to a country emergency department with a three-day history of worsening difficulty passing urine, frequency, and lower abdominal discomfort. He reports a 7-year history of benign prostatic hyperplasia with lower urinary tract symptoms. His medical history includes hypertension, type 2 diabetes, osteoarthritis, and previous deep vein thrombosis. His medications include metformin, perindopril, apixaban, and paracetamol.

On examination, he appears mildly uncomfortable. His pulse rate is 82/min, blood pressure 128/81 mmHg, respiratory rate 18/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 36.7°C. His abdomen is soft with mild suprapubic tenderness. Digital rectal examination reveals an enlarged, smooth prostate. Post-void bladder scan shows a residual volume of 400 mL.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Commence tamsulosin and arrange review in one week
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with acute-on-chronic urinary retention secondary to benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH), as evidenced by his significant post-void residual volume of 400 mL and progressive lower urinary tract symptoms.

Urinary catheterisation is the most appropriate immediate management step to relieve the retention and prevent bladder overdistension. This provides immediate symptom relief and allows time for planning definitive management. Assessment should be made with history and examination regarding the difficulty of catheterisation in an outpatient setting, in more complicated cases referral in to a emergency department for urology input may be required.

Commencing tamsulosin alone without catheterisation would be inappropriate in established urinary retention. However, alpha blockers should be started after catheterisation and before attempting a trial of void, typically after 2-3 days.

Urgent transurethral resection of prostate is not necessary as the first step. While this patient may require surgical intervention, the immediate management is bladder decompression with a catheter.

Prophylactic antibiotics are not routinely indicated for urinary catheterisation in an afebrile patient without signs of infection. They may be considered in high-risk patients or if the catheter will remain in place long-term.

Outpatient urology referral will likely be necessary but is not the most appropriate next step when faced with significant urinary retention requiring immediate relief.

After catheterisation, the patient should be commenced on an alpha blocker and have a trial of void in 2-3 days. If this fails, or if there are other concerning features, urological referral would then be appropriate for consideration of definitive surgical management.
learningPoints: Acute-on-chronic urinary retention in benign prostatic hyperplasia requires immediate bladder decompression
Alpha blockers should be trialled prior to any patient undergoing a trial of void, in the context of benign prostatic hypertrophy
Post-void residual volume greater than 300mL indicates significant urinary retention requiring intervention.
references: Serlin DC, Heidelbaugh JJ, Stoffel JT. Urinary retention in adults: evaluation and initial management. American family physician. 2018 Oct 15;98(8):496-503. Available online at ; https://www.aafp.org/pubs/afp/issues/2018/1015/p496.pdf

Yoon PD, Chalasani V, Woo HH. Systematic review and meta-analysis on management of acute urinary retention. Prostate Cancer and Prostatic Diseases. 2015 Dec;18(4):297-302. Available online at ; https://www.researchgate.net/profile/Henry-Woo-2/publication/280241346_Systematic_review_and_meta-analysis_on_management_of_acute_urinary_retention/links/564c64c208ae020ae9f9a6c4/Systematic-review-and-meta-analysis-on-management-of-acute-urinary-retention.pdf
